top_categories.workout_context: When someone attacks on mixed terrain, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
At mile 60 of 81, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
Viana Granfondo's mixed terrain rewards steady threshold power. Surging and recovering costs more watts than just holding.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Viana Granfondo's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before March.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Viana Granfondo. Train it until it feels boring.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 81 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 81 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
Long rides with hard efforts mixed in. Simulates what Viana Granfondo's mixed terrain actually does to you.
Viana Granfondo's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 81 miles.
Tempo is Viana Granfondo's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 81 miles on mixed terrain. Train it until it's automatic.
At Viana Granfondo, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
The aerobic base that lets you ride mixed terrain at tempo for 81 miles without cracking. No shortcuts.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Viana Granfondo’s 81 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: Moderate altitude at Viana Granfondo. Arrive 2–3 days early. Reduce intensity expectations by 5–10%. Hydrate aggressively.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on VO2max, Durability, and TT Threshold to prepare you for 81 miles of mixed terrain in Viana do Castelo, Alto Minho, Portugal.
